diagrams.title: Thread Library Classification Architecture
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing the classification of thread libraries into user-level and kernel-level categories with examples
elements: Top box: Thread Libraries (main category)
Two main branches below: User-Level Thread Libraries, Kernel-Level Thread Libraries
User-Level branch examples: POSIX Pthreads (user implementation), GNU Portable Threads
Kernel-Level branch examples: Windows Win32 Threads, Linux Threads, Solaris Threads
Dotted line showing the boundary between Application Space and Operating System Kernel
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to categorize thread libraries based on their implementation level and understand which libraries are available on different platforms

diagrams.title: Threading Models Architecture
diagrams.description: Three block diagrams showing the three main threading models: Many-to-One, One-to-One, and Many-to-Many
elements: Many-to-One Model: Multiple User Threads -> Single KLT -> One CPU (shows all user threads mapped to single kernel thread)
One-to-One Model: Each User Thread -> One KTL -> Multiple CPUs (shows direct mapping)
Many-to-Many Model: Multiple User Threads -> Multiple KTL (pool) -> Multiple CPUs (shows flexible mapping)
Labels: User Space boundary, Kernel Space boundary, Thread Library, OS Kernel
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how user threads are mapped to kernel threads in different threading models and the implications for concurrency and parallelism

diagrams.title: Pthread API and System Interaction Flow
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing the relationship between application code, pthread library functions, and the operating system kernel
elements: Application Layer: C program with pthread_create(), pthread_join(), pthread_mutex_lock() calls
Pthread Library Layer: Implementation of pthread functions (libpthread.so, pthread.dll)
System Call Interface: Functions that transition from user mode to kernel mode
OS Kernel: Scheduler, Thread management unit
Arrows showing: Application calls library functions -> Library makes system calls -> Kernel manages threads
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how thread libraries interact with the operating system and the path from user-level thread operations to kernel-level execution

diagrams.title: Thread Lifecycle State Diagram
diagrams.description: State diagram showing the lifecycle of a thread from creation to termination with various states
elements: States: New/Ready, Running, Blocked/Waiting, Terminated
pthread_create() arrow: New -> Ready
Scheduler dispatch: Ready -> Running
pthread_exit() / return: Running -> Terminated
Blocking calls (I/O, mutex wait): Running -> Blocked
pthread_join(): Blocked -> Ready (when thread completes)
Time slice expiry: Running -> Ready
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize the different states a thread can be in and understand the transitions triggered by various thread operations

diagrams.title: Process vs Thread Structure
diagrams.description: Comparative block diagram showing the memory structure of a single-threaded process versus a multi-threaded process
elements: Single-threaded process: Code section, Data section, Heap, Stack, Thread (single unit)
Multi-threaded process: Code section (shared), Data section (shared), Heap (shared), Stack 1 (Thread 1), Stack 2 (Thread 2), Stack 3 (Thread 3)
Highlighted differences: Each thread has its own stack, all threads share code, data, and heap
Labels indicating: Thread-specific resources, Process-wide shared resources
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the key difference between processes and threads in terms of resource sharing and memory layout

charts.title: User-Level vs Kernel-Level Threads Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Aspect, User-Level Threads, Kernel-Level Threads. Rows: Implementation, Management, Context Switch, Blocking, Scalability, Examples. Key differences: ULT managed by user-level library (fast, no kernel involvement), KLT managed by OS kernel (true parallelism, blocking doesn't affect others), Pthreads is kernel-level, Green threads are user-level

charts.title: Pthreads vs Windows Thread API Comparison
charts.content: Side-by-side comparison of POSIX Pthreads and Windows Thread API functions: Thread Creation (pthread_create vs CreateThread/_beginthreadex), Thread Termination (pthread_exit vs ExitThread), Thread Joining (pthread_join vs WaitForSingleObject), Mutex (pthread_mutex_lock vs EnterCriticalSection), Semaphore (pthread_sem_wait vs WaitForSingleObject on semaphore)

charts.title: Thread Creation and Execution Flow
charts.content: Flowchart: Start -> Define thread function -> Allocate thread attributes (optional) -> Call pthread_create() -> Thread created with ID -> Parent continues -> Child runs independently -> Parent calls pthread_join() -> Parent waits -> Child completes -> pthread_join returns -> Both continue -> End

charts.title: Thread Library Functions Classification
charts.content: Classification chart organizing pthread functions into categories: Thread Management (pthread_create, pthread_self, pthread_equal, pthread_join, pthread_detach, pthread_exit), Mutex Operations (pthread_mutex_init, pthread_mutex_lock, pthread_mutex_unlock, pthread_mutex_destroy), Condition Variables (pthread_cond_init, pthread_cond_wait, pthread_cond_signal, pthread_cond_broadcast), Thread Attributes (pthread_attr_init, pthread_attr_setdetachstate, pthread_attr_setstacksize)

branches: Definition: API for creating and managing threads
Types: User-Level (Green Threads, GNU Portable Threads), Kernel-Level (POSIX Pthreads, Windows Threads, Java Threads)
Pthread API: Creation (pthread_create), Synchronization (mutex, semaphores, condition variables), Management (join, detach)
Operations: Thread creation, Thread termination, Thread joining, Thread synchronization
Advantages: Responsiveness, Resource sharing, Economy, Scalability on multi-core
Challenges: Synchronization needed, Complex debugging, Race conditions, Deadlocks
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of thread libraries from types and models to API functions and challenges

branches: Thread Management: pthread_create(), pthread_self(), pthread_equal(), pthread_join(), pthread_detach(), pthread_exit()
Condition Variables: pthread_cond_init(), wait(), signal(), broadcast()
Read-Write Locks: pthread_rwlock_init(), rdlock(), wrlock(), unlock()
Thread Attributes: pthread_attr_init(), setdetachstate(), setstacksize(), setschedpolicy()
mindMaps.purpose: Focuses specifically on Pthreads as the primary standard thread library, organizing all major functions by category for quick reference
